entries.intro: The new tax collector near {subject_focus_the} has been taking more than his share. Everyone knows it, but nobody's willing to say it to the right people.
details: He marks down one number in his ledger and collects a different amount. Seen it myself twice now.
The surplus doesn't go to the treasury. He's got a private lockbox he keeps under guard at the inn.
He hired two men from out of town to watch his door. They don't talk to anyone and they don't look friendly.
reactions: The baker tried to confront him and found her permit to sell bread suddenly needed "renewal." Funny how that works.
I'm not brave enough to say it louder than a whisper. Last person who complained found their stall shut down.

statCheck.pass: I did some quiet asking around. His appointment came with a letter from someone high up, but the seal on the letter was wrong: slightly different from the real one.
statCheck.fail: His guards keep a tight circle. Believe me, others have tried to get a look at those ledgers.

entries.intro: The council near {subject_focus_the} voted to redirect the irrigation channel last month. Funny thing: it now waters the chairman's fields first and everyone else gets what's left.
details: Three families downstream have had their crops wilt. The chairman's wheat is the tallest it's ever been.
The vote was five to two. The three who swung it all got new fences built the week after. Paid for by nobody in particular, apparently.
reactions: It's not even subtle. That's the part that gets me: they don't bother hiding it because they don't think anyone will push back.
The downstream families are talking about pooling coin to dig their own channel. Shouldn't have to come to that.

entries.intro: There's a merchant near {subject_focus_the} who's been buying up land from families in debt. Offering a fraction of what it's worth and pressuring people to take it.
details: He targets anyone who owes money to the mill or the grain store. Waits until they're desperate, then makes his offer.
Three families sold this season alone. He's got more acreage now than the village commons.
I heard he's got an arrangement with the grain store: they call in debts early when he picks a target.
reactions: The families don't talk about it. They're ashamed, and he knows that keeps them quiet.
I tried to warn the last family before they signed. The merchant sent someone to have a word with me. Polite, but the message was clear.

statCheck.pass: I got hold of one of the sale contracts. There's a clause buried at the bottom: the merchant gets mineral rights too. He's not after the farmland. He's after what's underneath it.
statCheck.fail: The families who sold won't share the details. Too embarrassed or too scared, I think.

entries.intro: The guard captain near {subject_focus_the} has been looking the other way when certain wagons come through at night. Wagons that don't stop at the checkpoint.
details: They come once a week, always after midnight, always the same two covered wagons. The guards on duty suddenly find reasons to be elsewhere.
One of the younger guards asked about it and got reassigned to latrine duty for a month. Nobody's asked since.
reactions: I don't know what's in those wagons, but if it were legitimate, they'd come through during the day like everyone else.
The captain's living better than his salary explains. New boots, new cloak, and he's drinking at the expensive tavern now.

statCheck.pass: I followed one of the wagons at a distance once. It went to a warehouse at the edge of town that's supposed to be empty. It was not empty.
statCheck.fail: I have tried staying up to spot them myself. Either they do not come every night or I doze off at the wrong time. Hard to catch ghosts.

entries.intro: The healer near {subject_focus_the} used to treat everyone for what they could afford. Then someone from the capital visited, and now she charges triple and only sees certain people.
details: The visitor left her a supply of rare herbs. The kind you can't get without connections. Now she's dependent on his shipments.
She won't treat anyone who's spoken against the trade guild. It's not official policy: she just always seems to be busy when they come by.
She looks tired. Not greedy: tired. Like she's doing something she doesn't want to do.
reactions: I don't blame her entirely. When someone has leverage over your livelihood, your choices narrow fast.
The people who can't afford her now are going without treatment. A child broke his arm last week and the family had to set it themselves.
